question: A computer's CPU contains a Program Counter (PC). What does the PC store?

options.A: The result of the most recent calculation
options.B: The address of the next instruction to be fetched
options.D: The number of instructions executed so far

misconceptions.A: PC stores computation results — confuses PC with accumulator
misconceptions.C: PC tracks the current decode stage — confuses PC with IR
misconceptions.D: PC counts total instructions — misunderstands its role as a counter

question: Which bus carries the memory address of the data or instruction the CPU wishes to access?

misconceptions.A: Data bus moves addresses — confuses data bus with address bus
misconceptions.B: Control bus handles addressing — invents addressing role for control bus
misconceptions.D: System bus is a single unified bus — fails to distinguish individual buses

question: During the Fetch-Decode-Execute cycle, when does the Program Counter increment?

options.A: After the instruction has been executed
options.C: After the instruction has been fetched

misconceptions.A: PC updates after execution — delays increment to end of cycle
misconceptions.B: PC increments during decode — confuses increment timing with decode
misconceptions.D: PC pre-increments before fetch — reverses the sequence entirely

options.A: The CPU writes data to the wrong memory address
options.B: The requested data is not found in cache and must be fetched from RAM
options.C: Cache memory becomes full and data must be evicted
options.D: The cache and RAM contain conflicting versions of the same data

misconceptions.A: Cache miss means a write error — confuses miss with memory addressing fault
misconceptions.C: Cache full equals a cache miss — confuses eviction with a miss event
misconceptions.D: Cache miss means a data conflict — invents a consistency error
